details.ground_truth: It's been a year since Katie Holmes filed for divorce from Tom Cruise, and apparently Cruise is feeling sentimental. A source tells UK magazine Look the actor sent Holmes an "emotional letter" looking to, at the very least, be friends again, Australia's News Network reports. "It's like he's finally recognized that Katie will always be the love of his life," the source says. Even so, "It won't have been an over the top letter—it has been a year and I'm sure Tom will have been aware that Katie wouldn't be interested in running straight back into his arms." Sources close to Holmes say she was "touched" by the letter, which doesn't sound promising for Cruise. "Tom would no doubt take her back in a second, but I don't think that's what Katie wants," says one. (Just to keep things in perspective, we'll point out that Look is the sort of magazine that also runs headlines like, "Royal Bodyguard Warns: 'Kate Middleton's Baby Is In Danger!'") In other news, Huffington Post notes that it's Cruise's 51st birthday.

**Canadian Billionaire Couple Found Dead in Their Toronto Mansion**

Barry Sherman, a Canadian billionaire and founder of the generic pharmaceuticals giant Apotex Inc., and his wife Honey Sherman, a philanthropist and socialite, were found dead in their Toronto mansion on Friday, December 15. The couple's bodies were discovered by a real estate agent who had been helping to sell their North York home, which was recently listed for $6.9 million.

**Investigation and Cause of Death**

The Toronto police initially classified the deaths as "suspicious" but later announced that the homicide team was leading the investigation. The cause of death was determined to be "ligature neck compression," meaning strangulation. The police stated that there were no signs of forced entry into the home and no note left behind to explain what had happened.

**Murder-Suicide Theory**

Some news outlets reported that the police were investigating the case as a murder-suicide, but the couple's family and friends disputed this theory. In a statement released on Saturday, the family said, "Our parents shared an enthusiasm for life and commitment to their family and community totally inconsistent with the rumours regrettably circulated in the media as to the circumstances surrounding their deaths." They also accused the police of being irresponsible for leaking the theory to the media.

**Tributes and Condolences**

The couple's death sent shockwaves through the Toronto community, with many friends, colleagues, and acquaintances paying tribute to their memory. Toronto Mayor John Tory and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau were among those who offered their condolences. Barry Sherman was remembered as a dedicated workaholic who built a business empire from scratch, while Honey Sherman was described as a dedicated philanthropist who was always willing to lend a helping hand.

**Background on the Couple**

Barry Sherman, 75, was a Canadian billionaire with an estimated net worth of $4.77 billion, making him the 15th-richest person in the country. He founded Apotex in 1974 and had a reputation for being a shrewd businessman. Honey Sherman, 70, was a philanthropist and socialite who was involved in various charitable causes, including the Baycrest Foundation and the York University Foundation.

The couple's death has left many questions unanswered, and the investigation is ongoing.
details.ground_truth: The investigation into the mysterious deaths of a billionaire and his wife is now being led by homicide detectives, police say—suggesting the deaths of Barry and Honey Sherman were either murder-suicide or a double murder. Police, who have described the deaths as "suspicious," revealed Sunday that the cause of death for both Shermans was "ligature neck compression," a form of strangulation, the CBC reports. Sources have told the media that the couple were found hanging near an indoor pool in the basement of their Toronto mansion. Police say they haven't found any sign of forced entry, or a note that could explain what happened to 75-year-old pharmaceutical tycoon Barry Sherman and his 70-year-old wife, both renowned philanthropists. The couple's four children have rejected what they say are "rumors" that the deaths are being investigated as a murder-suicide, and many of the influential couple's friends agree. "There is absolutely zero debate in my mind, this was a double homicide," says Canadian Sen. Linda Frum, a longtime friend of the couple, per the New York Times. The Globe and Mail reports that just days before the bodies were found, Honey Sherman was enthusiastically making plans for their annual trip to Florida, emailing friends that she would be traveling to the state on Dec. 18 and Barry Sherman would join her a week later. The couple also sent out invitations last week to a dinner party at their winter home in Palm Beach.

**Egypt's Parliamentary Elections and Hosni Mubarak's Trial: A Turbulent Time for the Country**

Egypt is in the midst of a significant transition, with the country's first parliamentary elections since the ouster of former President Hosni Mubarak taking place in the final round. The elections, which began in November 2011, have seen a surge in support for Islamist groups, particularly the Muslim Brotherhood's Freedom and Justice party. The party is expected to consolidate its gains in the final round, which covers nine provinces, including the North and South Sinai governorates.

The Muslim Brotherhood's strong performance in the elections has raised concerns about the country's future direction, with some analysts suggesting that the party could win a clear majority of the seats in the full Parliament. This would enable the Brotherhood's party to govern without forming a coalition, reducing the clout of ultraconservative Islamists and liberal parties.

The elections are taking place against the backdrop of Hosni Mubarak's trial, which resumed on January 2, 2012. The chief prosecutor in the trial accused Mubarak of imposing "tyrannical rule" and devoting the last 10 years of his three decades in power to ensure his son, Gamal, would succeed him. Mubarak is charged with complicity in the killing of more than 800 protesters in the crackdown on a popular uprising that forced him out of office on February 11.

The trial has been bogged down in procedural matters, including a demand by lawyers representing the victims that the presiding Judge Ahmed Rifaat be removed. The trial is expected to continue for several more days, with the prosecution delivering its case against Mubarak and 10 other defendants.

The country's military rulers, who have been in power since Mubarak's ouster, have said they intend to retain the authority to hire and fire a prime minister to run the interim government. This has set up a potential confrontation between the new Parliament and the military rulers.

The economic situation in the country is also a concern, with growth slowing as the unrest deters tourists and investors. Foreign-currency reserves declined by 44% to $20.2 billion in the first 11 months of 2011, and the benchmark EGX 30 stock index lost 49% last year, making it the third-worst performer among world markets tracked by Bloomberg.

Overall, Egypt is at a crossroads, with the country's future direction uncertain and the economy facing significant challenges. The parliamentary elections and
details.ground_truth: The final round of voting began in Egypt today, and it looks like the Muslim Brotherhood could walk off with a controlling majority of seats in Parliament's lower house. The Muslim Brotherhood's Freedom and Justice Party, the country's mainstream Islamist party, had already won a high percentage of the seats awarded in the first and second rounds of voting. Two of the nine provinces voting today are Brotherhood strongholds, where some of the party's most well-known candidates are running, the New York Times reports. A plurality, not a majority, was originally predicted for the Freedom and Justice Party. A majority would allow the party to govern alone, but the Brotherhood has repeatedly said it plans to form a coalition government, and has aligned itself with more liberal parties rather than the ultraconservative Salafis, who are in second place with up to 25% of the seats so far. Also today, prosecutors began presenting their case against former Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak, Bloomberg notes. His trial resumed last week after a two-month break; state TV today showed Mubarak being carried to and from the courtroom on a gurney. "He deserves an end of humiliation and indignity," said the chief prosecutor, according to the AP.

**Summary of Recent News**

A Toyota Corolla driven by Nabila Altahan was suspended from a telephone pole's guy wire in Mendon after she reacted too quickly to a GPS unit's instructions to turn around. The incident occurred on Wednesday evening around 8 p.m. across from Sugar & Spice Restaurant. 

Altahan and her passenger were not injured in the incident, according to State Police. The car left the lane of traffic while westbound on Route 4 in Mendon after the GPS device gave sudden directions to turn around, as the car had passed its intended destination. 

A witness at the miniature golf course reported seeing the driver jump out of the car as it was suspended from the pole. State police and the Rutland City Fire Department responded to the scene, with Ray Ault, who happened to be driving past the scene around the time of the accident, providing an account of the incident.
details.ground_truth: Police in Vermont say a car ended up almost vertical when the driver swerved quickly in response to her GPS ordering her to "turn around." The car was suspended almost vertically on guide wires attached to a utility pole (see a photo here) in Mendon on Wednesday night, reports the AP. Police say 30-year-old Nabila Altahan of Dorchester, Mass., was headed west on US Route 4 when she passed her intended destination and reacted quickly to the instructions, leaving the road at a significant enough speed to propel the vehicle up the wires. She wasn't injured. (This driver followed her GPS into a lake.)
